translated: When did you celebrate them? On April twenty-sixth. Uh-huh, yes, and this year, God willing, we are going to celebrate it as if it were the fiftieth anniversary, the golden wedding anniversary, right? *laughs* Going to Benidorm is my passion, it's something I like, but you also get tired when you go so many times. Uh-huh, you already know everything there, so why go there? A place I haven't seen, I mean, well, what I want to say is yes, but in winter, the only place with an atmosphere is in Benidorm, you know that. But everyone goes there, and you can't walk through the streets, it's all full of people. Uh-huh, yes, in winter you can't go anywhere, that's what happens around here, around the Mediterranean, there's always a lot of people. *laughs* *snaps tongue* *laughs* *nods* Then there comes a time when, after four, five, or six years or more, in summer we started closing in the afternoons, and then we had more free time. But until then, I worked for a little while. I was a hairdresser, but when I married him, I learned the trade, and the dogs are getting on my nerves. *laughs* Both of them, it's okay. Yes, it is a problem because I'm listening for the phone, thinking it's going to ring. They're so annoying, for God's sake. *laughs* The neighbors call to complain. We had a very good teacher, the best teacher around here in Valencia, and then he took us, and we went to Pollos. *looks at* We started in Foyos, I think, no, Meliana, or in Meliana, and he charged, of course, he charged each of us, so we gave him some money. You know what happens, and then he lived precisely around there, near Pérez Galdós, almost in front of where we had the butcher shop, and as I said, we went to a lot of places, we went on excursions, we danced everywhere. He was a guy for NGOs, we went to Jaén. What a trip he wants us to take in September, which we do every year. We don't go every year; we have a house that was my father's, then we tore it down, so we are, hmm, we were, well, now it's just me, two girls, three, three, and me, four, and we tore down the house that was my father's, and the... We made everything new, and there is a beautiful house there in Jaén. It says yes *trn laughs* that you have some memory lapses now that your memory is *haedno trn laughs*. During the year, people work here and there and don't go to the village, but when the festivals come, since they practically only have one festival a year, uhm, when the festivals arrive, well, no one works anymore. They close everything, and everyone goes to the festivals. No one works, and everyone dedicates themselves solely to the festivities. In the morning, they have a procession. When the procession ends, they go to the square, eeh, the music plays, and everyone dances. But in the morning, they do it in the morning. They tie it to the four corners, as they call it, yes, but they only have a procession one day. The other days, what do they do in the morning? I stay at home. Well, then they tie the bulls at the entrance of the bulls. Ah, it horrifies me. I stay at home, and he goes, and there is also an esplanade where you can see at least more than four, five, or six kilometers.

Text_participant: En un lugar de la mancha de cuyo nombre lo quiere acordarme . 1020_5344 No ha mucho tiempo que vivía un hidalgo del orden . 6065_10030 Lanza en artillero a dar antigua rocín fla hidalgo corredor una olla de algo más que baja que carnero salpicón las más noches duelos y quebrantos los sábados retejas los viernes un palomino de añadidura a los domingos consumían las tres partes de su hacienda . 10320_19489
translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman of the rank of a gentleman, with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a lean horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more than beef stew, hash most nights, eggs and bacon on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

Sure, here's the translation of the provided Spanish text to English:

"Well, and tomorrow morning we have to come here to the doctor, after the doctor what do we do, do we go to the countryside or what? No, that's you who sets the pace, but we'll have to take out the animals we have at home. You see, we have four dogs there, and I understand that I also take walks because the dogs are there at home. So, you have the dogs so you can take walks, sir? No, because it's normal, he does it for me because I take walks and he takes walks too, haha, and that's it. It must be a big house, right, to have four dogs? No, it's just that the dogs, some out of pity, others because we raised them, and well, my son can tell you, not me. I was working, of course, that's why I say, but who brought you because I was at work? My grandson picked me up and brought me here. And who else, uh, well, who else? My grandson brought me here, and who else was in the car? Uh, uh, oh Paloma, yes, Paloma, my daughter-in-law. Oh, the poor thing also has her work, taking care of grandpa, and with the ointments, and this and that, that's why I say she has her work too. And look, and we tell it, as they say. Well, and in the afternoon, do we have to take a load of firewood to the restaurant or what? What do you mean, do we take a load of firewood to the restaurant in the afternoon? Oh, well, that's your business. Yes, but you'll have to help me load it. Of course, I'll help you whenever I can, I help with what I can, but since it's so little what I can do with this thing with my hand, this is from the accident in Picassent or Picassent at the cabin, uh-huh. And what is there to take care of there, what do we have there to take care of, plants? Well, moving arms, moving this, moving that, and I, since I have the misfortune that the train got me, I can't do what everyone else does there, and of course, I have a good time there. And music, but let's see, here it says they take a nap, so they all go to sleep or what? Where, here, uh, in the..." At school, we each lie down however we want on those chairs that are put there for everyone, and there's a lot of noise with people dragging them around, of course, to get a spot. That way, they don't lose their keys. This is pointless because I keep mine here, and since I don't have any bad habits, haha, I just go on like that.

And in brisca, what do you play with? We call it the malilla, the ace, and then the malilla, which is like the second most valuable, worth ten points, the king, hmm. No, well, we play with the horse, the two, or in another town, the three, you know. So, the ace is worth eleven points, the other ten, the horse, no, the king four, the horse three, of course, the king is the most valuable, I say. Well, then we play brisca differently, laughs trn laughs. When they think it's ready, they put it on for us, and then we comment on things, uh-huh. This is from May, yes, of course, and I suppose the day center won't be open in August either. In August, no, in August, no. What are they going to do in August? What are we going to do, uhg? If you ask my husband, he'll tell you right away because he goes to the village, which is in Teruel, the village. Ah, Manzanera, I don't know if you've heard of it, Manzanera, yes. Well, this is a hamlet, but I imagine we'll go up. So, do you go with your daughter, or do you go alone to your house there? Eh, at least we'll go up because I got my license and then left it at that. Ah, yes, I mean, she's the one who drives, of course. Yes, she got it years ago, I got it, yes. And what car did you have, maybe a six hundred? No, a six hundred that broke down, oh, it was an old car too. Ah, so you bought it from, oh, what a mess, who am I? You are María Teresa, you're my daughter. Ah, okay, that's why trn laughs. We could do that, of course, we could gather all the siblings in one place, right? Because everyone goes their own way, even though they're already retired and all, you know. But no, the one I mentioned, I said my brother, Uncle Jo, my son trn, I'm going to get my license just like that, oh, because you say, wow, I don't have to do this, well, yes, if everyone else does it, well, that's it, laughs trn. Well, nothing, well, look, at Pepsi Cola, there was a second cousin of mine, we were family, a... There, when there was more work in the summer, he had the opportunity because otherwise he would have worked in construction, meaning he has never really been unemployed. He also helped people in the fields there, of course, because in Castellar there are people who work in agriculture and such, and if they needed him, he would go too. And then, when he retired, and I had to go to the center where the bus passes by, we moved here, and we already had the house here, of course.

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. Text_participant: En un lugar de la mancha de cuyo nombre no quiero acord que no ha mucho . 100_7000 No hay mucho tiempo que vivía un hidalgo de los de lanza el astillero a adar antigua recién la y gargo corredor una olla de algo más vaca que carnero salpico las más noches duelo Duero y quebrantos los sábados y en tejas los viernes y los viernes que se lo anuncia al punto grado . 7440_14339 Algún palomino de añadiduras los domingos consumía las tres pata partes de su hacienda . 47810_58270
translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to recall, not long ago there lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a lean horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, hash most nights, eggs and bacon on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

He showed him the watch he's wearing. "Show me, where did you get that?" "They give these away now," he laughs. "Yes, where?" "Where did they give it to you? Who gave it to you?" "Right there, next to the tracks, next to the tracks," he laughs. "It's stuck in his head, he won't forget it. That guy, no, no, but what's that for?" "Well, it's a very simple thing, because you could take it out with your hand," he shakes his head. "No, you can't, but you can't take this out. It's for if you fall in the residence and so you don't go out the door." "Yes, yes, but I don't fall, not much. When? Not much, they've called us several times saying you've fallen." "But who called you from the residence?" "But it doesn't hurt, if it doesn't hurt, it wasn't anything important." "No, because right now these days, well yes, but I had that thing stuck in my head about that guy, about the food, about everything, because the guy would come and lie in bed, uh-huh, and what did he do? What did he do? Well, he wouldn't get up until the next day." "Well, everyone does what they want, but does he take your food?" "Does he take your food?" "I don't criticize the food." "No, does that man take your food?" "No, the food there at the convent," he looks at him. "Does he take your food?" "They don't take anything from me, man, because he has a cane, like Pilar," he laughs. "Because he has the cane, right? He comes and helps, girl," he laughs. "But you've eaten very well today, haven't you?" "Of course, everything's been great," he laughs. "If I had caught that guy, I would have thrown him out the window," he laughs. "No, no, eating something, like a sandwich." "Oh, okay, but for working, well, there are plenty of candies, so if you fancy one, eat it," he looks at him. "Do you eat any?" "No, you're lying, no, my mouth is a mess." "And what's that from? The candies?" "Pss, everything contributes, or from my father's inheritance, he doesn't have any of his own teeth." "It's not necessary to take it out," he laughs. "It's not necessary to..." Here's the translation of the provided Spanish text into English:

No, not me, not me, they're yours, you've paid for them, they're all yours *laughs* if you have such good teeth, you can eat a chocolate later, no, I don't eat fancy stuff like that, you see that man again, and I have him in my mind, in my mind and at the table, at the table too, at the table, no, not here, I mean at the residence, he sits at the same table, not me, I'm in my place, eh, and I eat there in my place, where, but what I'm saying is that the shameless guy sits there, tututú, to gobble, to gobble, they're going to give us a hard time, eh, and today I didn't go, no, not today because I told my son, I said, if he doesn't leave here, eh, without a whistle and nothing, he's going to get a beating, but why not, don't even think about it, eh, well, but don't even think about it, but do you think it's right to do what he's doing, well, there are people in charge there to tell him what they have to say, but not with beatings, no, not with the stick, not with beatings *laughs* with the stick to do more harm, no man, of course, of course, to do more harm, show him *laughs* the farmhouse of Alba, it's just that the farmhouse, I right now remember that guy with the pipe, remember the orchard, the one who smoked a pipe, ah the guy, I don't remember *laughs* this and this, this and this, what's from the Cañada river over there, and the river over there too, and there in the middle, you, eh, in the middle, you, in the middle until the water went away *laughs* it carried little, little water, well, it didn't take long to go away, the water didn't take long to go away, eh *laughs* ugh Pilar will be the one who remembers a lot, Pilar wasn't born, oh no *shakes his head* no, neither was I, no, a little, but I wasn't born yet, well, you're right, uhhum, uhhu." translated: In a household in La Mancha, I have read Don Quixote, and whose name I do not wish to recall, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. He had a pot with more beef than mutton, hash most nights, scrambled eggs and bacon on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and some pigeon as an extra on Sundays, consuming three-quarters of his estate.

translated:
And she makes dinner, yes of course, because she goes to play at the club in the afternoons. She doesn't have a flat rate, but of course, so what, everything goes out of her head, everything, everything. It's been a long time since we've noticed it, ugh, yes, yes, yes, I noticed it a lot last year. We were already like this, yes, or a little worse, but with the medication, it's been going quite well for us, yes, because there were other things that she did even worse, but well, it makes you very sad. I don't want to cry, of course, but it's normal to feel this way now. Look at this, this phone, well, we bought it for her just recently because the other one, well, the charger was a bit complicated for her, and there was no way, and this one is simpler. It's not that it's simpler, or if not, I charge it for her, and she had to, well, to talk, she had to open it and press here. Yesterday, among other things, I solved it for her, and now she just has to open it, uh-huh, so at least now she can carry it, and even if she doesn't know how to call, if someone calls her, of course, look, looks at trn, how it opens, let's see how it opens, see, like this, there, and the little window to the ear, yes, of course, and little by little, we're solving the issues, yes, yes, making life easier, of course. It seems unbelievable, eh, well, yes, it's a shame. Besides, she has been very active, she hasn't stopped, well, and she doesn't stop. I haven't stopped, and I've been working a lot in the field, a lot, a lot, since I was little, right, when you came from Córdoba, ugh, man, we came here, and to work here, I started picking potatoes and things like that, we worked like wolves, all day, because that was in the morning, and by noon, I was already here at home, and she made the food that I had prepared for her because she was little, but she managed her own things, let's see, laughs trn, you have to go to work, ah, because like Saturdays and Sundays, some Saturdays, they didn't work anymore, but then they didn't work, eh, you have to go to work on a Saturday, and I say, but that's how it is, the potatoes had to be picked in the mornings, and it didn't matter if it was Saturday, like Sundays, no, not Sundays, but Saturdays, yes, we went sometimes, we got together at least. The four or five of us, no more, at least six or seven, were picking potatoes, and we were all from here, from this same street, and it seemed like I was going somewhere else, maybe for something else *laughs* it was more, more, it was from the north, north, yes, yes, from the south, south *laughs* a very strong clash, wow, from the south, south, and it has always been like that, working here and working in the village, where we also worked in the fields before here, before, before, we worked a lot there. What village are you from? I am from Córdoba, but I am from Fuencubierta, next to La Carlota, next to La Carlota, ah, so we worked there. It is very close to Seville, yes, it is almost closer to Seville than to Córdoba, yes, yes. I was just thinking if La Carlota was the last village before Jaén or the first before Seville because when I drive, wow, without kids, you are repopulating Spain *laughs* but also had many miscarriages, no, but once I went there to Barcelona, to Seville, and I was with her, and there, I don't know, the days, and she just wanted me to stay and stay to help her, of course, with so many kids *laughs* of course, she had two apartments, right, and she still has them *laughs* *laughs* *laughs* I also see her, I also, I just, what happens is that yesterday I was there so, so comfortable that when I got up, I said, oh, but if it's already passed, and I went to the kitchen to make myself a coffee, and what happens to you with the coffee, the coffee that I can't, I can't figure it out, aaa, to put the, the, this, the coffee, she makes it now, and I almost don't even know how to make it anymore, yes, we have broken how many coffee makers, oh, coffee makers, coffee makers, I am breaking them all *laughs* why, what do you do to them?

So first, with my father, and then when I got here to Valencia, I was also lucky that a relative needed, um, someone who knew how to operate a type of machinery, yes, it was milling machines or the lathe and all that, and I think I was there for about nine years, as long as it lasted, the primitive would be the primitive, about nine years, and from there, a recession came, yes, in Salamanca, that's where the first time we saw each other, laughs, I said I was a captain at one time, I was the captain of the parade, I was everything, I was the queen, I was on the board, and I was the first female captain in the village, and you keep that little thing, and I would see and say, if I could convince the whole tribe, laughs, of course, because I'm the festive one, well, and little Pablo is also very festive, yes, he has the party in his veins, little Pablo seems to be very festive, he's more of a fallero, fallero, he's a fallero, he's festive for anything, laughs, but of course, here he has to be a fallero, festive there and here a fallero, laughs, but very well, very well, and well, we went out, it was very good, it was two days lived, yes, it's true because there was a choir, do you remember, yes. translated:
We talk about your brother Pepe. Pepe, who is married, is married to Cochín and lives in Benimaclet next to, what's it called, the thing with the quereguilles. No, not the quereguilles, no, no, the quereguilles warehouse. The warehouse, you know, where Pachá used to be. Well, um, laughs, you know, where Pachá was, yes, the nightclub that was a nightclub later. No, no, Uncle Pepe lives there. Uncle Pepe lived next to the warehouse, okay, nods, okay. And what can you tell me? And Aunt Conchín, what about her? Aunt Conchín, well, she's, well, a bit under the weather, but laughs, like you, is she worse or better than you? How is she? laughs, laughs, laughs, playing there, playing a lot. That's good. Where did your husband work? He worked at Lladró. And what happened to your father? Tell José Luis because he doesn't know. Well, he took the car, the car that dad used, the motorcycle, okay, and he took the car and it hit him on the motorcycle. And how many years ago was that? Many years, laughs, he doesn't talk anymore. And the kids were all small, and she took care of all of us. And now it's our turn to take care of her. She's a courageous mother. We didn't have this house; it was a house on a road that went to the cemetery. No, it's a bit confusing there. You got it mixed up. Yes, we lived in Pellis, we lived there when dad was around, and then when dad left, they came to live here, right? And living there in the neighborhood that was called, and here, no, she didn't live. No, that's it, mom, from there to here, the cemetery thing is from when you were young, when you were young and lived next to your mother. Ah, there in Benimaclet, uh-hum, nods, yes, then they bought this house later. Yes, I bought it to get married, laughs, laughs, that's it, this topic never fails, laughs, laughs. It was when you went to work, right, on Russafa Street, yes, looks at, from there to Ruzafa Street, ugh, the shameless guy would come out, laughs, what else, what else, laughs, I went through a lot, I don't remember, like your friend when you went down there to the street, eh, nods, to the corner, and the train would pass, the train would pass, nods, and the cars would honk at you. Here's the translation of the provided Spanish text to English:

"Did they ever scare you, do you remember or not? Yes, back in those days, my goodness, and it wasn't that long ago, not that long ago. And Paquita, how long has it been since we last saw Paquita? Quite a while, she's not doing well. Not very well, is she better or worse than you? I don't know, she's not well. Yesterday on the avenue, yes, and you can count on your fingers the times I've run into her, of course. And her sister, of course, she's not doing well. The one who's doing very well is Paz. Oh, but now I heard she was in delicate health. Oh yes, the other day, I don't know who it was, said she's not well, yes, she was very unwell. Oh yes, shakes her head no. And so now, who, if she went to pick up the grandchildren and everything, haven't you seen her going to pick up the grandchildren? I haven't seen her, and I never hear anyone talk about her around here. No, last week we talked with her. I nod. With Paz, yes, you're getting confused, dear. Well, because time passes very quickly for you here. Uhm, the days pass very quickly for you. I don't remember, girl, it's okay, it's okay, I don't remember. Have you gone for a walk? Now a few days will pass, and they'll tell me this guy has gone home, laughs. And I'll say, oh, now I don't know, now I don't remember, laughs. Do you realize those things? And I'll say, it must be true, but it's okay because he'll greet you, of course. Yes, he'll greet you, you don't need to remember anything. I say that the women should tell me, that they should tell you, listen, that guy, and I don't remember. This happens to me a few times, and that's what scares you, that they tell you. Now it wouldn't happen to me because I wouldn't be scared now. Oh no, huh, damn, laughs. That was when you were young, but I don't go out calmly anymore. Oh, okay, but you never go alone anymore, of course. So what's going to happen to you, dear, don't make things up. You know how many times they used to scare me, this is getting repetitive, laughs. You see there on the bike, on the Vespa, that I'm not there, you're not on any Vespa, in that chair, that's dad. If it's not you, that's not me. Oh girl, you just told José Luis that this guy, that man, is dad. Oh, it's you, it's you, that's dad all along, down there in the rocking chair, you've got it mixed up. Well, and what about that?" Here's the translation of the provided Spanish text to English:

"Everything they operated on, tell us about it, don't you remember what they operated on you for? What did they operate on you for? What was it, the stomach? No, no. No, they operated on your hip. The hip? Ah, which one is that? This one, this one, the hip. And oh, she fell to the ground. *shakes her head no* They didn't put stitches in me, no. Yes, a rod. After menopause, there's wear and tear and *laughs* *laughs* *laughs* dragging, smart one *laughs* *laughs* *laughs* Do you have any moments during the day when it bothers you? *looks at her* And look at my husband there, sitting in the hammock, in the rocking chair under the trees, how calm he is there. How happy he was. Wasn't he very happy? And have you been happy or not? Very, but he's gone. You all are left with me, huh. How old were you when dad left? I don't remember. Young, no? *nods* Yes, you were four already born. Yes, you were fifty, fifty years old. Four children were already born, four. And now how old are you? Sixty and how many? *laughs* Never knows, never. Eighty. *laughs* What was it, what was it this past month? Yes, but how many do you turn? I turn, yes, damn, just a few years ago, eighty-one. Eighty-one, yes."

translated: In a place in La Mancha, the name of which I do not wish to recall, not long ago I saw a nobleman of the kind who keeps a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. translated:
I like it, I like it, I'm going to come here to eat someday *laughs* *laughs* *trn laughs* but there are things I don't let slide, and she knows it. I might make a mistake because I've lost almost all my memory. The other day, I left my shoes there, and she scolded me, "Dad, put your shoes away." She understands that it's not intentional, of course. And you can tell the memory problems are noticeable when you can't remember someone's name or a word doesn't come to you. No, what happens is that I have this flaw, not a flaw, but I leave something and then I have to think about where I put it because if you get nervous, it's worse. Uh-hum, then your mind gets in such a state that you don't even know where you left it. So I think, well, if it's not there, it must be here, and if not, in the bed or the bathroom, the watch or the glasses. Many times she says, "Dad, your glasses are there." The other day, I was carrying my wallet and kept looking for it. *looks at trn* What was it, girl? What was it? I don't remember what it was. I don't know what it could be. I don't remember either, and my son said, "But Dad, you have it." He came to see me and said, "Dad, look where your wallet is, under the bench." The wallet was there. I had been going around the house. It's not that small, not big either, but that's fine. Uh-hum, damn, if it's not here, where could it be? Then I went to the bedroom because I used to sleep in that room, my room. But of course, since they are married now, they want a bit more space, to be more comfortable, and I don't mind where I sleep. Sometimes I go into the room because I've been sleeping in that room for more than thirty years. *looks at trn* Thirty years I've been sleeping there, of course, but as I said, I remember things from fifty years ago more than suddenly now. Yes, sir, because I had an accident more than twenty years ago, and I have a brother who, forty-two years ago, minus two months, had my son. So I remember that. I remember, I remember a lot, I remember when I was little, I remember the accidents I've had, a donkey that bit me too. She might not do it with bad intentions; it's just her temperament, and then she's a sweetheart, very clean and hardworking like no one else. I say this here and anywhere else. She has always been like that, while her brother has been the opposite, more laid-back and calm. But she, right now, goes to work, goes to bed with her husband, and you don't have to worry because he doesn't have to call her. However, maybe she does have to call him, just like what happened to me with my wife, may she rest in peace, the poor thing. But I never had to call her; she was very responsible with her work. Yes, of course, then, of course, the circumstances of life change, and you adapt, and the jobs too. I've been very lucky with jobs, well, not today because there are no jobs, nothing at all. I got married well, but poor, but very well, you know. I took care of my people, my things, and then I brought some money of my own after getting married. I brought fifty thousand pesetas after getting married, forty-five thousand pesetas for the furniture, and another thirty-five thousand that the bride brought. A little bit of money that we both had. Then we got married, and I was paying for a small house that was forty meters, it wasn't much, just a small area with four, six, eight, ten, or twenty houses. You don't remember that, of course not. I paid two hundred and fifty pesetas a month, and I was working with my brothers in Gandía. Then my sister-in-law saw these apartments here on the lower street because her aunt and my wife worked together in the same factory. Later, she had hip problems and couldn't climb the stairs anymore, so we moved here. But this one, I had to help the family a bit, and it cost me more because, of course, the factory brought in some Catalans, and the factory was... Here's the translation of the provided Spanish text to English:

"To hell with it, and then of course they fired everyone. The factory had been in ruins for at least four or five years or more, with all the machinery. I had to pay a hundred and something thousand pesetas for the apartment installment. So, of course, my wife was worried that they were going to take the apartment away from us. She started to get on my case, and I didn't argue with her. Sometimes I even cried, but she doesn't remember any of that because she was very young. Do you remember any of that? No, not at all. Maybe your brother does. I said they won't take the apartment away from us. Then, how are we going to pay for it? My parents couldn't lend us less than five hundred thousand. Look, go to your brother Ángel. I have two brothers, one named Ángel and the other Juanjo. Or if not, go to Juanjo. I said, okay, don't worry. The following week, we went to see the grandparents. I can still picture it. I felt very embarrassed to ask them for six hundred thousand pesetas that I needed for the apartment. So, what do you want? Come on, just tell me. I said, look, lend me six hundred thousand pesetas because I can't pay it. I could pay it, but I had to take her along, and she was unemployed, but she was getting a pittance from unemployment benefits. Well, maybe she was getting, I can't tell you the exact amount, but very little. So, I went there with her and said, come on, let's do this. He said, look, take your bankbook and send it to Benimamet, and I'll send my bankbook to your bank in Nazaret. And indeed, within a month, I had the six hundred thousand pesetas in the bank with my bankbook from there, and I made the transfer with her bankbook here."

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, hash most nights, duels and breakages on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

translated: In a place of La Mancha, the name of which I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those who keep a lance in a rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. "But here I suppose they don't do house chores, right? You say they don't? Yes, of course, they send you. Do you bring your shirts? Of course, and do you mend them here? No, no, no, that won't be it. They might do something else, like, I don't know, like the mural over there of spring. Have you seen the mural over there, the poster? Maybe you did that too? I don't know, I don't remember. I've done so much that I don't remember anymore. Well, that's normal. *laughs* Yes, I do everything for him, whatever it is. I don't mind sewing for him. Uh-huh. And is he your only child? No, there are more, but they're in other places. *looks at trn* But tell the boy, the boy asks you. Look at him, he's another one. The one I live with is this one. Uh-huh. But if you have more children, he asks you. I don't know, José, Toni, how many do you have, my son? Right now, I'm not going to start counting if they're all over the place, each one on their own. But more or less, can you tell the boy? The boy asks you, Toni, Isa, how many are we? Do you remember more or less? Paco, I don't remember anymore. I've been like this for so long that I don't remember anymore. *laughs* trn You're dizzy from doing so much work. *laughs* trn *laughs* trn *laughs* *laughs* trn I've helped a lot, but now I'm fed up. Uh-huh, because it gets very tiring. Yes, you know how many stitches I've made in my life, and we keep going as long as we keep counting. I believe it, right? That's it. Do you remember more or less how many grandchildren you have? Not right now, I don't know. If I start counting them, maybe I can figure it out. Go ahead, start counting them, you count them. *laughs* trn *laughs* *laughs* trn The gaps, the gaps, you fill them right away, and the same question, and you're left without an answer. It makes a mess there or whatever."

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of stew with more beef than mutton, hash most nights, scrambled eggs and bacon on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

translated: A place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, there is not much time to live. The nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, a lean hack and a greyhound for coursing, a pot of more beef than mutton, splashed most nights with stew and scraps, lentils on Fridays, some extra on Saturdays, and on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his estate.

translated: In a place in La Mancha, the name of which I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those who kept a lance in the rack, an old shield, a lean horse, and a swift greyhound. A pot of more beef than mutton, salad on most nights, scraps and eggs on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

translated: In a place in La Mancha, there lived a man I do not wish to remember. Not long ago, there lived a nobleman, one of those who kept a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing.
